name: Drafting Amicus Interest Statements
summary: This skill produces the "Interest of Amicus Curiae" section of an amicus brief, guiding the LLM to establish institutional credibility, tether the amicus's interest to the specific legal question presented, and articulate concrete non-party impacts. It addresses key procedural requirements under FRAP 29 and Supreme Court Rule 37, embeds ethical guardrails around truthfulness, confidentiality, conflicts, and disclosure accuracy, and requires attorney verification for all rule-dependent statements and organizational facts before filing.
content:
# Drafting the Interest of Amicus Curiae Statement

## 1. Opening Context

This skill guides an advanced LLM through drafting the "Interest of Amicus Curiae" section of an amicus brief. That section is not a ceremonial preface; it is the credibility gateway that tells a court why the amicus is a legitimate, useful participant and why the court should trust the amicus's perspective when deciding the specific legal issues presented. In modern appellate practice, where courts are increasingly burdened by a high volume of amicus filings, the Interest Statement must immediately signal that the filer possesses unique information, expertise, or a perspective that the parties themselves cannot or will not provide. When done well, it increases the odds the brief is accepted (or at least read closely) and frames the amicus as a reliable source of practical consequences, technical context, industry realities, or doctrinal perspective that the parties are structurally unlikely to present.

Poor execution has real costs. A vague, generic interest statement invites skepticism, can trigger motions to strike or requests to disregard the brief, may undermine the perceived integrity of the amicus, and produces the kind of redundant "me-too" brief that many courts have criticized. Good execution, by contrast, is concise, precise, issue-tethered, and candid about the amicus's identity, mission, expertise, and stake—without exaggeration. Professional standards implicated include the relevant court rules governing amicus filings (notably Federal Rule of Appellate Procedure 29 and Supreme Court Rule 37), general duties of candor to tribunals under Model Rule of Professional Conduct 3.3, and confidentiality and authorization constraints when describing the amicus's activities and stakeholders.

This skill focuses exclusively on drafting the Interest Statement itself. It does not draft the summary of argument, the argument section, or a full motion for leave to file. However, it assumes the Interest Statement will fit within a compliant amicus filing package and will not contradict any required disclosures elsewhere in the brief.

## 2. Understanding the Context and Purpose

The "Interest of Amicus Curiae" section exists because amicus participation is discretionary and instrumentally justified: courts accept amici to the extent they assist the court beyond what the parties provide. Supreme Court Rule 37, paragraph 1, states that an amicus brief should bring to the attention of the Court relevant matter not already brought to its attention by the parties—this is the gold standard for what an Interest Statement must promise. In most appellate settings, the operative question is not whether the amicus has "standing" in the Article III sense, but whether the amicus has a legitimate interest and can provide useful information, perspective, or expertise. Courts and clerks read this section early to answer four practical questions: (1) who is the amicus; (2) why is it filing in this case; (3) what qualifies it to speak; and (4) how the decision will affect the amicus or the broader community it represents.

Strategically, the Interest Statement should be drafted with the same discipline as the merits argument: it must be tethered to the exact questions presented and the procedural posture. The best Interest Statements do not merely describe a mission in the abstract; they explain how the organization's work intersects the contested legal rule and how the ruling will predictably affect identifiable constituencies, markets, regulated entities, or public-facing systems. The audience is primarily the court (judges and clerks), but it is also opposing counsel (who may challenge the brief's propriety), the media (who may quote it), and the amicus's stakeholders (who expect accuracy and reputational care). The section sets a tone: it can signal neutrality (e.g., professional associations or academic centers), principled advocacy (e.g., civil rights organizations), or technical expertise (e.g., industry groups), but in all cases it should present an honest, restrained account that withstands adversarial scrutiny.

In the lifecycle of a matter, the Interest Statement is often the first thing a judge reads after the table of contents. If the amicus is a trade association, the interest may center on economic stability and regulatory compliance for an industry. If it is a group of scholars, the interest may be the integrity of a specific legal doctrine or statutory framework. If it is a civil rights organization, the interest may be the protection of a vulnerable class. The drafter must anticipate the court's skepticism—judges routinely ask, "Why am I hearing from you?" The Interest Statement must answer this by showing that the case's outcome will create precedent that directly impacts the amicus's mission or members in a way that the primary litigants might subordinate in their pursuit of a case-specific outcome.

## 3. Prerequisites and Information Gathering

Before drafting, the LLM must obtain sufficient information to produce an Interest Statement that is accurate, tailored, and disclosure-consistent. The LLM should request and review the following inputs, and if any are missing, it must ask the user for them before producing a final draft.

First, gather case context and procedural posture. Obtain the case caption, court, docket number, and jurisdiction; the procedural stage (e.g., merits briefing in an appellate court, en banc rehearing, certiorari petition stage, merits stage at the Supreme Court, or trial-level motion practice); the questions presented or issues on appeal; the decision below and the specific holding under review; and filing constraints such as word limits and deadlines.

Second, gather the amicus's identity and authority to file. Obtain the amicus's full legal name, entity type (e.g., 501(c)(3) non-profit, trade association, professional society), and headquarters location; whether it is filing alone or as part of a coalition; whether it has members, and if so, the nature of membership, membership size, and geographic or industry scope; the year it was founded (to establish longevity and a permanent stake in the subject matter); and internal authorization status (board approval or officer authorization) if needed for accuracy and governance. The LLM should also ask whether the amicus has any direct relationship to the parties—membership ties, funding, litigation support history, or contractual relationships—that may affect how the interest should be framed or require disclosure.

Third, gather mission and expertise facts that can be stated with confidence. Obtain the mission statement as used publicly (website, charter, annual report); core programs, research, standards-setting activities, or service delivery functions; relevant publications, policy statements, model codes, technical standards, or amicus filing history; the specific domain expertise that aligns with the legal issue (e.g., First Amendment and platform moderation; administrative law and rulemaking; public health and emergency powers; intellectual property and innovation incentives); and any empirical or operational experience the amicus has that will be referenced in the brief. A list of previous cases where the amicus has filed briefs or a summary of its history in legislative advocacy related to the issue can help establish a long-standing interest rather than a sudden interest in a single case.

Fourth, gather the concrete stake and predicted impact. Obtain information about who will be affected if the court adopts either party's position (members, clients, beneficiaries, industry participants, the public); the mechanisms of impact (compliance obligations, liability exposure, enforcement patterns, operational feasibility, chilling effects, resource allocation, safety outcomes); and whether the impact is immediate or longer-term, and whether it is unique to the amicus or widely shared.

Fifth, gather the parties' briefs if available. To ensure the amicus offers something different, the drafter should know what the parties have already argued. The amicus's interest often lies in collateral consequences, technical context, or broader systemic effects that the parties may downplay or lack incentive to present.

Sixth, gather disclosure-related information that must be harmonized with the Interest Statement. Depending on jurisdiction and court rules, amici may need to disclose whether counsel for a party authored the brief in whole or in part, whether a party or party's counsel contributed money intended to fund preparing or submitting the brief, and whether any other person made such a monetary contribution. These disclosures are required under Supreme Court Rule 37.6 and FRAP 29(a)(4)(E). The LLM should explicitly ask the user: who authored the brief; whether any party or party's counsel funded it; and whether any other contributors funded it. If the user cannot confirm, the LLM must flag the issue for attorney verification and avoid statements that could be inaccurate.

Because filing requirements vary significantly by court, the LLM must ask which court rules govern (e.g., U.S. Supreme Court, a specific federal circuit, a state supreme court, or a state intermediate appellate court). If the user does not know, the LLM should proceed with a draft that is substantively sound but mark it as requiring a jurisdictional compliance check and avoid making definitive claims about rule-based requirements.

## 4. Step-by-Step Drafting Process

### Step 1: Anchor the Interest Statement to the Procedural Posture and the Precise Legal Issue

Begin by identifying the exact decision the court is being asked to make and the doctrinal lever that will control the outcome. The purpose is to ensure the Interest Statement is not a generic biography of the organization. Judges and clerks are looking for relevance: why this organization, in this case, on this question. A strong practice is to mirror the language of the questions presented (or the principal issues in the opening brief) and connect the amicus's stake to the choice the court faces.

For example, if the case turns on the standard for preliminary injunctions in a regulated industry, the Interest Statement should identify why that standard affects the amicus's members' operations and compliance decisions. If the case turns on statutory interpretation, the Interest Statement should explain why the amicus has expertise with the statute's implementation, regulated community expectations, or administrability concerns. Explicitly mention the specific statute, constitutional provision, or common law rule at the center of the case—this shows the court the amicus has read the record and is not filing a generic brief recyclable in any similar case.

The following is illustrative language to be adapted to the specific case: "Amicus curiae [Organization] submits this brief to assist the Court in resolving whether [legal issue framed in the case's terms]. Because [Organization] [does work directly tied to the issue], the Court's resolution of this question will have significant consequences for [identified constituency] and for the practical administration of [system/market/regulatory program]."

Common mistakes include describing the organization's mission for a full paragraph without mentioning the legal issue, reciting broad values (e.g., "fairness," "good government") untethered to the legal question, or overstating the organization's stake in a way that implies an undisclosed party relationship or a direct financial interest that cannot be substantiated.

Adversarial awareness is important here: opposing counsel may argue the amicus is simply another mouthpiece for a party or that the brief adds nothing beyond what the parties already present. A tightly issue-anchored opening sentence reduces that attack surface by showing the court exactly what unique value the amicus contributes. Even where not strictly required by the governing rules, it is often strategically helpful to include one restrained sentence distinguishing the amicus's perspective from the parties' arguments (e.g., "The parties understandably focus on [X]; amicus addresses [Y]").

### Step 2: Describe the Organization with Legally Relevant Specificity

After the anchor sentence, provide a concise, fact-based description of the amicus: who it is, what it does, and how long it has done it. The goal is credibility and clarity. Courts distrust puffery; they value concrete indicia of expertise—membership size and composition, institutional role, standards-setting authority, research output, service footprint, or frontline operational experience.

Use specifics that are easily verifiable from public sources or internal records the amicus can produce. If the draft cites membership numbers, years of operation, geographic scope, or program scale, those facts must be correct as of a known date. If there is any uncertainty, qualify the statement or flag it for attorney verification.

The following is illustrative: "[Organization] is a nonpartisan [nonprofit/trade association/professional society] founded in [year] that represents [describe members/constituency] across [geographic scope]. Its work includes [two to four core activities], including [activity directly relevant to the legal issue], and it regularly provides [guidance/training/technical standards/research] to [stakeholders]."

Common mistakes include using vague descriptors ("leading," "premier," "renowned") without substantiation, including a long list of programs unconnected to the case, or failing to disclose coalition composition when multiple amici are filing together. Another error is focusing on the amicus's internal committee structure rather than its external role and mission—courts care about what the amicus does in the world, not its internal governance.

Adversarial awareness: if the organization is perceived as ideologically aligned with one side, overstated neutrality can backfire. It is better to be candid (e.g., "advocates for," "works to advance," "represents") than to pretend the amicus is neutral when its public posture suggests otherwise. Overclaiming objectivity can damage credibility with the court. Conversely, to preempt arguments that the amicus is merely a party proxy, emphasize the amicus's independent research, diverse membership, or history of taking positions that do not always align with a single side.

### Step 3: Establish Expertise That Maps onto the Doctrinal Choice Before the Court

Next, articulate why the amicus is particularly well positioned to help the court decide the legal question. Expertise is not just subject-matter knowledge; it is a bridge between real-world consequences and legal standards—administrability, reliance interests, institutional competence, downstream litigation effects.

Identify two or three "expertise pillars" that correspond to the legal issue. These might include operational experience implementing the statutory scheme at issue; technical knowledge of the regulated subject; longitudinal data or published research; professional ethical standards or industry norms; or experience with litigation patterns and remedial design. Then connect those pillars to the exact point the amicus will address in the brief's argument section.

The following is illustrative: "Amicus has particular expertise relevant to the Court's analysis of [issue], including (1) [experience pillar], which informs how [legal rule] functions in practice; and (2) [experience pillar], which bears on the administrability and reliance consequences of adopting [proposed standard/test]. Amicus submits this brief to provide the Court with [type of assistance—technical context, historical practice, empirical evidence, comparative approaches]."

Common mistakes include treating "expertise" as credentials alone (e.g., simply naming famous members or advisory board members) without explaining how that expertise helps the court answer the legal question. Another mistake is promising that the brief will provide empirical data or technical analysis and then failing to deliver in the argument section; the Interest Statement is a credibility commitment, and unmet promises erode trust.

Adversarial awareness: opponents may argue the amicus is not an expert in the relevant domain (e.g., a general policy group weighing in on technical regulatory matters). The Interest Statement should preempt this by being precise about the amicus's actual work in the domain and by avoiding overbroad claims. Where expertise is adjacent rather than direct, frame it honestly ("experience advising [regulated entities] on [subject]," "experience studying [phenomenon]") and connect it to the doctrinal inquiry.

### Step 4: Explain the Impact of the Ruling on Non-Parties with Concrete Mechanisms

Courts value amici most when they clarify consequences that the parties' incentives may minimize. This step translates doctrine into real-world effects. Identify who beyond the parties will be affected and how. The "how" should be described in mechanisms that sound like evidence, not conjecture: compliance costs, altered enforcement incentives, changed contracting behaviors, chilled speech, barriers to innovation, disrupted public programs, uncertainty in standards, or increased litigation volume.

A good method is to articulate a short causal chain from the legal rule to the consequences. For instance: "If the Court adopts [X] standard, then regulated entities will [do Y] to avoid liability; that will produce [Z] effect on [beneficiaries/market]." Frame the interest in terms of broad impact—courts are more likely to attend to an amicus that explains how a ruling will create a circuit split, disrupt established business expectations, or undermine a specific regulatory framework. Keep it plausible, bounded, and tied to the record or to widely recognized institutional realities.

The following is illustrative: "The Court's ruling will affect not only the parties but also [defined group], including [amicus's members/beneficiaries]. If the Court holds that [legal rule], then [mechanism], which would [practical consequence]. Because [Organization] works directly with [group/system], it has a concrete perspective on how the Court's articulation of [test/standard] will shape [behavior/administration]."

Common mistakes include asserting sweeping social consequences without support, using speculative rhetoric, reciting policy arguments that belong in the argument section, or failing to define the affected population (e.g., "the public," "all Americans") when a narrower, more credible constituency is available. Avoid arguing the merits of the case in this section; instead, argue why the resolution of the case is vital to the amicus and its constituency.

Adversarial awareness: exaggeration invites rebuttal. Opposing counsel may point out that the amicus's asserted harms are speculative or contradicted by record evidence. To reduce that risk, the Interest Statement should avoid specific empirical claims unless the brief will cite support and the authors can verify the sources. If the Interest Statement references research, phrase it in a way that is easy to substantiate ("has conducted research on…," "has published guidance regarding…") and ensure the underlying citations can be verified.

### Step 5: Tailor Tone and Framing to the Court and the Amicus's Role

Amicus Interest Statements must match the court's expectations and the organization's authentic posture. A standards-setting body, a professional society, or an academic center often benefits from a restrained "assist the Court" tone emphasizing technical clarity and administrability. A civil rights organization or a trade association may appropriately emphasize how the decision affects its constituency and core mission. Either posture can be persuasive if it is honest and consistent.

Draft with an eye toward judicial sensibilities: avoid partisan slogans, avoid attacking parties, and avoid rhetorical flourishes that read like fundraising copy or passionate closing arguments. The Interest Statement should read like a statement of institutional concern, not a political manifesto. If the amicus has a direct stake, say so plainly. If it is primarily offering expertise, say so plainly. If both, combine them, but keep the relationship intelligible.

The following illustrates a "neutral expertise" posture: "Amicus does not file to support any party as such, but to assist the Court by providing [technical/historical/industry] context relevant to the proper interpretation of [statute/constitutional provision]."

The following illustrates a "stakeholder advocate" posture: "Amicus supports [party position] because the decision below threatens to [consequence] for [amicus constituency], whom amicus serves through [programs/representation]."

Common mistakes include claiming neutrality while explicitly urging a result without acknowledging advocacy, using combative language that belongs in merits sections, or creating internal inconsistency (e.g., the Interest Statement claims the amicus represents a broad coalition but the brief later discloses substantial funding by one interested entity).

Adversarial awareness: the opponent may quote the Interest Statement to portray the amicus as biased or self-interested. Draft as though every sentence will be excerpted. That does not mean sanitizing the amicus's viewpoint; it means writing with disciplined accuracy.

### Step 6: Harmonize the Interest Statement with Required Disclosures

Even though disclosures may appear elsewhere in the brief (often in a separate section), the Interest Statement must not contradict them. If the Interest Statement suggests the amicus is independent but disclosures show substantial party funding or party authorship, the brief risks reputational damage and potential sanctions.

The LLM should avoid statements about independence, authorship, funding, or non-involvement unless the user confirms them; avoid implying that the amicus has conducted investigations or gathered data specific to the case unless that is true and can be supported; and avoid naming specific members, clients, or beneficiaries if doing so would violate confidentiality or internal policies.

When independence is confirmed by the user, the following standard disclosure language may be appropriate: "Pursuant to [Rule Reference], amicus curiae states that no counsel for a party authored this brief in whole or in part, and no person or entity other than amicus curiae or its counsel made a monetary contribution intended to fund the preparation or submission of this brief." This language must be treated as a formal disclosure that requires verification against both the applicable rules and the actual facts. The LLM should draft it only as bracketed optional language unless the user affirmatively confirms its accuracy and identifies the governing rule.

Common mistakes include copying standard disclosure phrases from other briefs without confirming facts, accidentally making the Interest Statement the only place disclosures appear when the governing rules require a separate disclosure section, or providing inaccurate funding or authorship information that could result in the brief being stricken.

Adversarial awareness: party-funded amici are common and not inherently improper, but lack of transparency is a litigation risk. Opposing counsel may seek to strike or diminish the brief by highlighting inconsistencies between the Interest Statement and the disclosures.

## 5. Critical Legal Standards and Compliance

Drafting an amicus Interest Statement implicates core procedural rules and professional duties.

On the procedural side, Federal Rule of Appellate Procedure 29 governs amicus filings in federal circuit courts. Rule 29(a)(4)(D) specifically requires a statement indicating the amicus's interest, and Rule 29(a)(4)(E) requires the authorship and funding disclosures. Supreme Court Rule 37 sets the standard for amicus participation at the nation's highest court: paragraph 1 states that an amicus brief should bring to the attention of the Court relevant matter not already brought to its attention by the parties. Rule 37.6 requires the specific disclosures regarding authorship and funding. These rules establish the procedural and substantive baseline, but the LLM must always direct the user to verify the current text of the applicable rules, as they may be amended, and state court rules may impose different or additional requirements.

On the ethical side, competence under ABA Model Rule of Professional Conduct 1.1 requires that counsel ensure the Interest Statement is accurate, properly tailored, and compliant with the court's procedural rules. Candor toward the tribunal under Model Rule 3.3 requires that the amicus be honest about its identity and its relationship to the parties—misrepresenting an amicus as independent when it is funded by a party violates this duty. Truthfulness in statements to others under Model Rule 4.1 applies to all factual representations about the organization's membership, activities, expertise, or motives. Confidentiality under Model Rule 1.6 requires care when describing the amicus's work with beneficiaries, clients, or members; if describing sensitive programs, investigations, or relationships could reveal confidential information, the statement should be generalized or the amicus should provide informed consent. Conflicts of interest under Rules 1.7 and 1.9 can arise if counsel represents parties or related entities whose interests are materially adverse to the amicus's position, or if the amicus's coalition includes members with divergent interests. The LLM should not attempt to resolve conflicts; it should flag potential conflict indicators for attorney review.

Malpractice risk concentrates in three areas. First, factual overreach: claiming expertise, membership, or impacts without support can be used to impeach the brief and counsel's credibility. Second, procedural noncompliance: filing an Interest Statement that suggests the amicus meets rule requirements when it does not can endanger acceptance and lead to the brief being stricken. Third, disclosure errors: incorrect independence or funding statements are a high-risk failure mode that can result in sanctions.

The mitigation strategy is disciplined sourcing (use only verified public or client-provided facts), explicit uncertainty flags, and mandatory attorney verification before filing. This skill does not provide legal advice. All outputs must be reviewed and approved by a licensed attorney responsible for the filing.

## 6. Quality Control and Review

Quality review of an amicus Interest Statement is less about stylistic elegance and more about credibility under scrutiny. The following review protocol should be applied before any draft is finalized.

First, test relevance: after reading only the Interest Statement, could a clerk accurately explain in one sentence why this amicus belongs in this case? If not, the statement is too generic.

Second, test issue tethering: does the Interest Statement name the specific legal question or doctrinal standard at stake, rather than merely describing the subject matter area?

Third, test non-redundancy: does the statement clearly articulate what unique perspective the amicus brings that the parties lack—such as technical data, historical context, specific industry impact, or the experience of a constituency not represented by the parties?

Fourth, test mission alignment: is the stated interest logically consistent with the organization's mission? If a labor union is filing in a patent case, the Interest Statement must clearly bridge that gap (e.g., how patent policy affects member employment).

Fifth, test factual support: every factual claim—membership numbers, years of operation, geographic scope, programs, publications, and asserted impacts—should be traceable either to a reliable public source (e.g., the organization's official materials) or to internal organizational records. If the draft includes any quantitative or superlative claims ("largest," "represents X million"), confirm exactness. If exactness cannot be confirmed, rewrite to a qualitative but accurate statement or flag it for verification. The LLM must flag all organizational facts for attorney verification.

Sixth, test consistency with disclosures: if the brief contains a separate disclosure statement, read it alongside the Interest Statement for contradictions in tone (e.g., "independent" vs. "funded by"), authorship claims, and affiliations.

Seventh, test adversarial resilience: assume opposing counsel will cherry-pick a sentence to argue the amicus is biased, unqualified, or improperly involved. Remove rhetorical overstatements, narrow causal claims, and ensure each sentence is defensible.

Eighth, test procedural compliance: confirm that any required disclosures under FRAP 29, Supreme Court Rule 37.6, or applicable state rules are present, accurately placed, and consistent with the Interest Statement's representations.

Citations are typically minimal in an Interest Statement; however, if any legal rule is referenced (e.g., consent requirements, disclosure requirements), the LLM must either cite the governing rule with a verifiable source or flag the statement for attorney confirmation. All output requires attorney review before use.

## 7. Jurisdiction-Specific Adaptations

Amicus practice is rule-driven, and the content of an Interest Statement is influenced by forum conventions. Because this skill is designed for multi-jurisdiction use, the LLM must adapt based on the court identified by the user and must not assert specific rule requirements without verification against current rule text.

In United States Supreme Court practice, Interest Statements are typically concise but formal. The Court expects the amicus to explain how the case has national importance and how the amicus helps the Court decide a question of law with broad implications. Supreme Court Rule 37.1 explicitly discourages briefs that do not serve this purpose. The well-known disclosure expectations regarding authorship and funding under Rule 37.6 must be addressed, and the LLM should draft an Interest Statement that can be paired with a compliant disclosure section.

In federal courts of appeals, amicus rules and conventions vary by circuit, and local rules may specify whether leave is required, when consent matters, and what disclosures must be included. FRAP 29 provides the general framework, but local rules supplement it. The Interest Statement should be especially focused on how the decision will affect non-parties within the circuit and why the amicus's perspective differs from the parties'. Some circuits, notably the Seventh Circuit, have been historically critical of redundant "me-too" amicus briefs. In such jurisdictions, the Interest Statement must go further in demonstrating that the amicus is truly independent and providing information the parties have not covered.

In state appellate courts, requirements can diverge substantially. Some states impose unique disclosure or motion-for-leave practices; others have distinct norms about whether amici may introduce facts outside the record. For example, California Rule of Court 8.520(f) requires the Interest Statement to also state whether the amicus has a relationship with any party beyond funding and to identify any other cases in which the amicus has filed on the same issue. In these settings, the LLM should draft the Interest Statement to minimize contestable factual claims and should flag any plan to rely on extra-record factual assertions for attorney review.

At the trial court level, while amicus briefs are rarer, the Interest Statement should focus heavily on "assisting the court" with complex factual or technical issues that the parties may be too biased or too resource-constrained to present clearly.

Across all jurisdictions, the safest substantive approach is to keep the Interest Statement tightly tethered to the issues presented; describe the amicus with verifiable facts; articulate concrete impacts without speculative exaggeration; and quarantine rule-dependent disclosure language into optional bracketed text pending attorney verification of the governing rules and underlying facts. When the user has not identified the specific court or the LLM cannot verify the current rule text, all rule references should be flagged for attorney confirmation before filing.
